coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of microalgae-based carbon dots for decoration of TiO2 nanoparticles in enhancement of organic dye photodegradation
coredata.dc:creator: Vu Nu, Truong Thi
Thi Tran, Nhu Hoa
Truong, Phuoc Loc
Phan, Bach Thang
Nguyen Dinh, Minh Tuan
Dinh, Van-Phuc
Phan, Thanh Son
Go, Seongmoon
Chang, Mincheol
Loan Trinh, Kieu The
Van Tran, Vinh
coredata.dc:description: TiO2 is a well-known semiconductor used widely in the photocatalyst field, but its photocatalytic applications are hampered by a fast electron–hole recombination rate and low visible light absorption due to a wide-band-gap energy. Herein, we present a simple, low cost, and green approach to obtain carbon dots from microalgae, namely microalgae-based carbon dots (MCDs), using an unprecedented microwave-assisted treatment. The MCDs were successfully decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The as-prepared composite exhibited a superior photodegradation of methylene blue, compared with pristine TiO2 (83% and 27%, respectively) under visible light irradiation. The MCDs in TiO2-MCDs serve as electron reservoirs to trap photoinduced electrons and as photosensitizers for the improvement of visible light absorption; both factors play an important role in the improvement of the TiO2 photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the as-prepared composite photocatalyst also exhibits high photostability and recyclability during the photodegradation of methylene blue. Therefore, this work provides an original approach to the development of environmentally friendly and highly effective photocatalysts for the treatment of various organic pollutants, which can go a long way toward ensuring a safe and sustainable environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
TiO2
Co-catalyst
Dye photodegradation
Microalgae
Microwave-assisted treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MCDs and TiO2-MCDs composite
Characterizations of MCDs and TiO2-MCDs composite
Impact factors on photodegradation efficiency of TiO2-MCD composite
Photostability and reusability of TiO2-MCDs composite
Proposed mechanism of photodegradation reaction of MB by TiO2-MCD composite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research (N.H.T.T and V.V.T) was supported in full or in part with a Kurita Asia Research Grant ( 20Pvn011-I40 ) provided by the Kurita Water and Environment Foundation and also supported by Vietnam National University in Ho Chi Minh City to Center for Innovative Materials and Architectures ( Laboratory for Optics and Sensing ) under grant NCM2019-50-01 . M. Chang was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2020R1I1A306849711 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of TiO2-MCDs composite.

Optical and morphological properties of MCDs: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and optical images of MCDs under daylight and UV light irradiation (inset); (b) Fluorescence spectra; (c) TEM image; (d) Size distribution.

Optical, morphological, and structural properties of TiO2-MCDs: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and optical images of TiO2-MCDs 0.3 under daylight and UV light irradiation (inset); (b) XRD pattern; (c) Raman spectra; (d) SEM image; and (e) SEM-EDS mapping images.
XPS analysis of a TiO2-MCDs composite with 0.3% w/w of MCDs.

Photocatalytic efficiency of the TiO2-MCDs composite in MB degradation: Photodegradation efficiency (a) and kinetic curves (b) at different concentration of MCDs; Photodegradation efficiency (c) and kinetic curves (d) at different pH of medium; Photodegradation efficiency (e) and kinetic curves (f) at different concentration of MB.
Photostability and reusability of TiO2-MCDs composite and pristine TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of MB.
Schematic illustration of photocatalytic mechanism exhibited by TiO2-MCDs: (a) Diagram of the DOS of TiO2-MCDs (b) MB degradation mechanism of TiO2-MCDs under visible light irradiation.
Summary of comparison of photo degradation efficiency of MB of different catalysts based on composites of TiO2 and carbon materials.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: R, removal efficiency (%); t, irradiation time; N-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs); plasmonic nanostructures (PAu/Ag).

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of microalgae-based carbon dots for decoration of TiO2 nanoparticles in enhancement of organic dye photodegradation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Truong Thi Vu Nu and Nhu Hoa Thi Tran contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiO2 is a well-known semiconductor used widely in the photocatalyst field, but its photocatalytic applications are hampered by a fast electron–hole recombination rate and low visible light absorption due to a wide-band-gap energy. Herein, we present a simple, low cost, and green approach to obtain carbon dots from microalgae, namely microalgae-based carbon dots (MCDs), using an unprecedented microwave-assisted treatment. The MCDs were successfully decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The as-prepared composite exhibited a superior photodegradation of methylene blue, compared with pristine TiO2 (83% and 27%, respectively) under visible light irradiation. The MCDs in TiO2-MCDs serve as electron reservoirs to trap photoinduced electrons and as photosensitizers for the improvement of visible light absorption; both factors play an important role in the improvement of the TiO2 photocatalytic activity. Furthermore, the as-prepared composite photocatalyst also exhibits high photostability and recyclability during the photodegradation of methylene blue. Therefore, this work provides an original approach to the development of environmentally friendly and highly effective photocatalysts for the treatment of various organic pollutants, which can go a long way toward ensuring a safe and sustainable environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, photocatalysis has emerged as a promising approach that has attracted huge attention in environmental cleaning and energy conversion (Hoffmann et al., 1995; Kubacka et al., 2012; Zou et al., 2001). Several semiconductor photocatalysts have been applied to wastewater treatment, including TiO2, ZnO, CdS, CdIn2S4, and WO3. Among these photocatalysts, TiO2 is still the most used semiconductor, contributing approximately 25% to the photocatalyst field due to its chemical stability, high photocatalytic activity, and environmentally friendly properties (Bui et al., 2018, 2020; Nguyen et al., 2020). However, the photocatalytic applications of TiO2 are hampered by its wide-band-gap energy and a fast electron–hole recombination rate (Ma et al., 2014; Schneider et al., 2014). Many chemical or physical methods have been investigated for modifying TiO2, including, cationic/anionic doping, coupling with other semiconductors, and surface loading with co-catalysts to overcome these drawbacks (Ma et al., 2014). One of the most promising strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance is the development of co-catalysts for the modification of the TiO2 surface (Naldoni et al., 2019). The formation of heterojunctions and the deposition of co-catalysts on the TiO2 surface can significantly enhance the charge separation through the formation of an interfacial Schottky barrier (Chen and Mao, 2007). In general, the noble metals, i.e., Pt, Au, and Pd, have exhibited the most effective co-catalytic activity. However, their commercial applications have been limited because of the high associated cost. Therefore, it is necessary to develop novel co-catalysts with high photocatalytic activity and low cost.

Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional (0D) quasi-spherical particles <10 nm in size, are a potential candidate for the improvement of the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 due to their suitable bandgap, unique electron donor/acceptor properties, and excellent electron transfer characteristics (Ke et al., 2017; Lee et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018; Mahmood et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2013, 2016). Moreover, it has been demonstrated that composites of CDs and TiO2 exhibit a high efficiency in the degradation of organic dye pollutants such as methyl orange (Deng et al., 2021), rhodamine B (Zhang et al., 2013), and methylene blue (MB) (Ke et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016). In the past decade, CDs have been prepared using several conventional methods including the hydrothermal treatment (Deng et al., 2021; Ke et al., 2017), electrochemical synthesis (Yu et al., 2014), arc electrophoresis (Xu et al., 2004), ultrasonic treatment (Li et al., 2011), and pyrolysis (Zhu et al., 2013). However, these approaches had inherent drawbacks of too high an energy consumption, complex process requirements, high temperatures, extended durations, and high cost (Kou et al., 2020). Furthermore, CDs were often formed from conventional precursors such as citric acid monohydrate, carboxylates, carbohydrates, graphene, and carbon nanotubes. Recently, green synthesis methods have attracted attention as a promising alternative for the preparation of CDs from renewable carbon precursors such as cabbage, grass, banana juice, plant leaves, sweet pepper, and apple juice (Chahal et al., 2021). Therefore, exploring and developing advanced and facile synthetic routes to fabricate CDs from renewable resources are still necessary. We recently developed a microwave-assisted method for the simple synthesis of CDs from Opuntia humifusa leaves (Moon et al., 2020). It has been demonstrated that the microwave technique has the advantages of a shorter duration, a low-energy consumption, and a simple process (Li et al., 2018).

In this work, we developed microalgae-based carbon dots (MCDs) as a co-catalyst for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2. A TiO2-MCDs composite is prepared by decorating MCDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles through a simple two-step microwave treatment method. This TiO2-MCDs composite was employed as an efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of organic MB under normal visible light. The inclusion of MCDs induced an improvement in the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. The photo-physical and structural properties of the MCDs and TiO2-MCDs were systematically investigated and evaluated. The as-prepared photocatalysts displayed enhanced photocatalytic degradation of MB after coupling MCDs due to the upconversion of photogenerated electrons and holes. Subsequently, a reasonable mechanism was proposed to illustrate the critical roles of MCDs in the photocatalytic process.

Methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S·3H2O, MB), titanium (IV) butoxide, and ethanol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Blue-green algae powder tablets were obtained from eVitamin company, USA. In this study, all the chemicals used were analytical grade and utilized without further purification.

The morphology and elemental composition of TiO2-MCDs were identified via field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM 4700) using an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer. The anatase/rutile structures of TiO2 and the TiO2-MCDs were determined using an X-ray Diffractometer, Miniflex (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). The chemical compositions and chemical states of the elements in the TiO2-MCDs were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Sigma Probe, Thermo VG Scientific, USA). UV–Vis absorbance spectra of the samples were recorded using a UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (Model-2550, Shimadzu) in the range of 200–800 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MCDs and TiO2-MCDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: MCDs were prepared using a recently reported microwave-assisted method, with some modifications (Fig. 1 ) (Hoang et al., 2021; Moon et al., 2020). Briefly, the microalgae tablet (5 g) was first dissolved in DI water (10 mL), followed by stirring at a high speed for 2 h at room temperature to make a homogenous suspension. Next, this microalgae suspension was treated by microwave at 800 W for 5 min. The solid powder obtained was ground to a fine powder, which was the final MCDs product.

TiO2 nanoparticles were prepared according to the method recently published by Bui et al. (2018). Firstly, titanium butoxide (TB, 1 mL) was added to ethanol (40 mL), followed by DI water (0.25 μL). The milky suspension that formed was stirred for at least 12 h and centrifuged, washed with ethanol and dried to form a white TiO2 solid. Thereafter, the white solid was calcinated at 350 °C for 2.5 h in a furnace to form TiO2 nanoparticles.

The TiO2-MCDs nanoparticles composite was synthesized by mixing different ratios of MCDs with TiO2 nanoparticles under support using a microwave treatment. In brief, TiO2 nanoparticles (1 g) were dispersed in DI water (10 mL) and thereafter, MCDs (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, or 0.5 g) were inserted. The mixture solution was treated by microwave at 800 W for 90 s, and the obtained solid was cooled and powdered to the final product of the TiO2-MCDs composite.

The photocatalytic activity of the catalysts was evaluated by the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation at room temperature (approximately 25 °C). Running water was stirred magnetically into a Petri dish and maintained a constant temperature in the reaction system. Specifically, MB (100 mL, 10 ppm) solution and TiO2-MCDs composite (100 mg) were mixed and vigorously stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach an adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Next, the reaction solution was continuously irradiated using a visible light source (500 W Xe lamp with a UV cutoff filter (k > 420 nm) for 2 h. Samples (1.5 mL) were withdrawn at 20 min intervals, and filtered to remove the solid phase. The absorbance of these filtrates was measured at 664 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to determine the remaining quantity of MB. The same procedure was applied to the blank reaction that was used as a control, but without adding any TiO2-MCDs catalyst.

The kinetic photocatalytic performance can be evaluated using the simplified Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L–H) kinetic model (also called pseudo-first-order kinetic model) shown in Eq. (1):
(1)
− ln
C
C o
= k t ,
where Co is the initial concentration of MB, C is the concentration of MB at time t, and k is the apparent first-order reaction rate constant.

Recycling experiments were performed via the photocatalytic decomposition of MB using the TiO2-MCDs photocatalyst. After each cycle, the samples were collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with water. A fresh MB solution (100 mL) was then injected to perform the next cycle. Each sample was evaluated repeatedly over 4 cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of MCDs and TiO2-MCDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: MCDs were synthesized using the carbonization process of microalgae-based C sources under microwave-assisted treatment (Fig. 1). The success and efficiency of the microwave-assisted method in the preparation of MCDs is ascribed to the ability of the polar molecules in a microalgae solution to absorb microwave radiation and convert it into heat, via dielectric heating (de Medeiros et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 2 a (inset), the microwave treatment of microalgae at 800 W for 5 min yielded a dark green color under daylight and a blue color under UV light irradiation (365 nm), indicating the generation of carbon nanodots (Amjad et al., 2019). The obtained MCDs were initially confirmed by the UV–visible absorption spectrum. The UV–Vis spectra of MCDs dispersed in water exhibit an absorption peak at λ max = 268 nm and a broad shoulder starting from 300 nm, which are induced by the π–π* transitions of the CC (aromatic carbon) bonds and the n–π* of CO (carbonyl) and C–N bonds, respectively (Fig. 2a). These peaks lead to the strong emission peaks of the MCDs at 450 nm (Fig. 2b). In addition, MCDs displayed a small absorption peak at 610 nm, corresponding to a weak emission peak at 660 nm. This phenomenon is due to the presence of chlorophyll pigment, which remained in the MCDs (Domenici et al., 2014). The PL excitation spectrum exhibits two peaks at 450 and 660 nm, indicating the two-photon emission property of MCDs. TEM images confirmed that the as-prepared MCDs have spherically shaped particles with an average size of <8 nm and a uniform and narrow distribution (Fig. 2c and d). The particle size distribution was obtained by counting approximately 100 particles, and it indicated that the MCD particles are distributed in the range from 1.5 to 7 nm with an average diameter of 5 nm. The results reveal that we have successfully synthesized monodispersed microalgae-based MCDs with a relatively uniform distribution through a facile microwave-assisted method.

The as-prepared MCDs were decorated on the surface of TiO2 to form TiO2-MCDs photocatalysts via a two-step process (Fig. 1). The solution containing MCDs and TiO2 was again microwaved to ensure the MCDs were anchored on the surface or interface of titania. To gain insight into the characteristics of TiO2 combined with MCDs, the morphological, structural, and optical properties of TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs were investigated. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis spectra of TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs. A broad absorption edge in the UV–Vis spectrum of TiO2, assigned to its intrinsic bandgap absorption, which is useful to its photocatalytic property, was observed at approximately 280 nm (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). For TiO2-MCDs, the optical bandgap energy decreased, leading to a slight shift of the absorption edge to the visible region with an enhanced absorption intensity, due to the C atoms occupying the interstitial sites in the TiO2 lattice (Liu et al., 2017). Thus, the bandgap energy of TiO2-MCDs is smaller than that of TiO2, indicating that the photocatalytic performance of TiO2-MCDs is greater than that of TiO2. The absorption intensity in the range of 300–800 nm was much stronger than that of TiO2 and is related to the existence of the π−π* transition of the CO bonds and the n-π* transition of the CC bonds in the MCDs; this is in accordance with the UV–Vis data of the MCDS. Fig. S1 illustrates the valence band and bandgap energy value of TiO2-MCDs, which are 1.6 and 2.91 eV, respectively. The bandgap energy of TiO2 (∼3.2 eV) (Ke et al., 2017) was reduced upon TiO2 being decorated by the MCDs. These findings confirmed that the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 can be improved due to increased light absorption and electron–hole generation.

The powder XRD patterns of the synthesized pure TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs containing different quantities of MCDs (0.1–0.4 %w/w) are presented in Fig. 3b. Generally, pristine TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs exhibit identical patterns with four main peaks at 24.9, 37.3, 48.5, 53.7, 55.1, and 62.5°, assigned to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), and (204) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively (Li et al., 2014). However, the intensity of these peaks decreased after embedding different concentrations of MCDs, indicating the successful decoration of MCDs on the surface of TiO2. These results demonstrated that the presence of MCDs slightly influenced the crystal structure of TiO2. The average TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs crystallite sizes, calculated by Scherer's Formula, are approximately 85.2 and 91.2 nm, respectively, indicating that the TiO2-MCD powder comprises nanocrystal particles. The Raman spectra of pristine TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs containing varying MCD contents in the spectral range 900–3300 cm−1 are shown in Fig. 3c. The Raman spectrum of the TiO2 has no peak in this range, whereas TiO2-MCDs composites show two peaks at ∼1352 and ∼1607 cm−1 corresponding to the D- and G-bands, respectively. G-bands are characteristic of sp2 hybridized carbon materials, while D-bands relate to the disordered band associated with structural defects in graphene (Ferrari and Basko, 2013). Moreover, the G band has a higher intensity than the D band, which indicated the presence of sp2 graphitic carbons with sp3 carbon defects in the MCDs structure. We can therefore infer that the MCDs were successfully incorporated onto the TiO2 particles in the TiO2-MCDs composites.

The surface morphology of TiO2-MCDs was further investigated via SEM and SEM-EDS analyses (Fig. 3d–e and Fig. S2). The SEM images indicated that the pristine TiO2 consists of uniform microspheres and small particles comprising crystallite sizes ranging from 50 to 200 nm, and TiO2 also exhibits a smooth surface (Fig. S2). The TiO2-MCDs composite exhibits a uniform distribution of MCDs over the TiO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 3d). After being incorporated with MCDs, the TiO2 morphologies exhibit a negligible change, wherein the surface of the TiO2 microsphere became rough and the proportion of dispersed particles increased. These phenomena indicate that the MCDs produced surface defect sites, which can be expected to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. In addition, EDS mapping results verified that the TiO2 particles were successfully covered by the MCDs (Fig. 3e). The pristine TiO2 displays only two elements, Ti and O, while the TiO2-MCDs exhibit four main elements, including Ti, O, C, and N. From these results, it may be concluded that the TiO2-MCDs composites were successfully fabricated.

XPS analysis was performed on the TiO2-MCD composite comprising a 0.3% w/w concentration of MCDs, to further illustrate the interaction of MCDs with TiO2. The full-scale XPS spectrum confirms the existence of Ti, O, C, and N in the TiO2-MCD composite because of the existence of the peaks of C1s (284.3 eV), N1s (398.8 eV), Ti2p (458.1 eV), and O1s (529.7 eV) (Fig. 4 ) (Li et al., 2008). The deconvoluted C1s XPS spectrum presented four typical peaks at 284.6, 285.3, 286.2, and 287.9 eV, which are assigned to the C–C/CC (graphite), C–N, C–O–H, and CO bonds, respectively, that originated from the MCDs. The high-resolution O1s spectrum peaked at 529.4, 530.9 and 532.5 eV, and these peaks are attributed to the Ti–O, O–C–O and O–CO bonds, respectively (Cong et al., 2011). The Ti2p XPS spectra exhibits peaks at 458.2 and 463.9 eV observed in the TiO2-MCD composite for Ti(2p3/2) and Ti(2p1/2), respectively. The deconvoluted peaks of N1s at 399.8 eV indicated the existence of C–N bonds, suggesting the presence of amine functional groups in the MCDs molecules. In addition, there was no peak corresponding to the Ti–C bond found in the XPS spectrum of TiO2-MCD, which suggested that the MCDs are not decorated on the TiO2 particles as a dopant (Hou et al., 2015; Ke et al., 2017). Thus, it may be concluded that the coupling of MCDs with TiO2 occurs via the formation of the Ti–O–C bond (Vijayan et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2014). Moreover, the formation of the TiO2-MCDs heterojunction composite was also created by the close electrostatic interaction between the functional groups (N–H or O–H) of the MCDs and TiO2 (Deng et al., 2021; Ke et al., 2017). This linking enables photoexcited charge carriers (holes or electrons) to transfer through the contact interface.

The photocatalytic performance of the TiO2-MCDs was evaluated by the degradation of MB under visible light irradiation. The batch scale experiment is illustrated in Fig. S3. The MB photodegradation by the composite comprising different MCD concentrations (0.05–0.5 %w/w) was first investigated at a fixed concentration of MB (10 ppm). All the TiO2-MCDs composites exhibited a higher efficiency of degradation of MB than the control pristine TiO2, and the concentration of MCDs significantly influenced the photodegradation efficiency of MB (Fig. 5 a). Specifically, the photocatalytic performance of the TiO2-MCDs composite increased rapidly with the increase in the MCDs concentration from 0 to 0.3 and is stable when the concentration of MCDs exceeds 0.3 over all the contact durations. For example, the efficiency of the photodegradation of MB increased from 0 to 27% and 83% after 120 min reaction times for pristine TiO2 and TiO2-MCDs 0.3, respectively. Thus, it may be inferred that the MCDs play a critical role in enhancing the MB photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 (Chen et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2014). This rapid increase in the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2-MCDs is attributed to the increased light absorption and improved separation efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers (Tran et al., 2021). However, increasing the concentration of the MCDs to 0.4 and 0.5% does not increase the photocatalytic performance, but it can result in a slight decrease. It has been demonstrated that the quantity of CDs would influence their distribution on the surface of the metal oxide (Chen et al., 2010). At a high concentration of MCDs, the excess MCDs can form a continuous layer covering the TiO2 surface, hindering light harvesting and charge transfer (Fig. S4). Moreover, the aggregation of MCDs on the surface of TiO2 also decreases the separation efficiency of the photoinduced electron–hole pairs (Wu et al., 2015). To obtain an accurate comparison, the kinetic photocatalytic performance was performed and the apparent first-order reaction rate constant (k) of the TiO2-MCDs in the degradation of MB was calculated (Fig. 5b and Fig. S5). The results indicated that the combination of TiO2 with MCDs promotes the reaction kinetic process where TiO2-MCD 0.3 displayed a reaction rate constant 6.5 times higher than that of pristine TiO2. Therefore, it may be concluded that the TiO2-MCD comprising 0.3% MCDs is the optimum sample, that achieved the highest photocatalytic efficiency using the lowest quantity of photocatalyst. The UV–Vis spectra and an optical image of the degradation of MB using TiO2-MCD 0.3 showed the complete removal of the dye color (Fig. S6). Similar samples will be selected for further evaluations relating to the impact factors on the photocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact factors on photodegradation efficiency of TiO2-MCD composite
ce:section.ce:para: From the results of the XPS spectra, it is evident that the surface of the TiO2-MCD composite contains charged functional groups (amine or –OH); thus, the pH is an important factor that can influence the MB degradation process. We evaluated the impact of pH on the photocatalytic efficiency of the TiO2-MCDs at four pH values (pH 4, 6, 8, and 10). An increase in the pH from 4 to 10 led to a significant increase in the photodegradation of MB (Fig. 5c). This indicated that the TiO2-MCDs exhibited a higher efficiency of the photocatalytic activity under alkaline conditions. Specifically, the MB photodegradation exhibited the lowest fraction at pH = 4.0 (65%) and it gradually increased to 75, 80, and 83% at pH 6.0, 8.0, and 10.0, respectively. Accordingly, the degradation kinetic rate constant (k) at pH 10 also increased by half that of pH 4.0, 12.35 × 10−3, and 8.1 × 10−3 min−1, respectively (Fig. 5d and Fig. S7). This phenomenon can be explained based on the functional groups present on the surface of TiO2-MCDs, which are mainly responsible for the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes throughout the photodegradation reaction (Chen and Yan, 2011; Jia et al., 2012). Under alkaline conditions, the electron transfer between the MCDs and TiO2 can be facilitated by the higher electronegativity of the functional groups. The increased photodegradation could also be ascribed to the concentration of OH·radicals, which is relatively higher in the alkaline medium (Abd Elkodous et al., 2020; Elsayed and Gobara, 2016). On the other hand, the lower efficiency of photodegradation in the acidic medium may be ascribed to the instability of the TiO2-MCDs because of a cathodic dislocation of the valence band position, weakening the oxidation capability of the holes.

The effect of the initial MB concentration was further investigated using three different MB concentrations, i.e., 10, 20, and 30 ppm. Overall, when the MB concentration was higher in the medium, lower degradation rates were exhibited (Fig. 5e and f). For example, the photodegradation efficiency of the TiO2-MCDs composite for the 10, 20, and 30 ppm MB concentrations gradually decreased from 83 to 62% and 51%, respectively, after 120 min of irradiation. Similarly, the degradation rate also decreased from 13.5 × 10−3 to 7.4 × 10−3 min−1 and 6.2 × 10−3 min−1 (Fig. S8), which implies that the MB photodegradation speed decreased by more than half when the MB concentration increased from 10 to 30 ppm. This phenomenon can be induced by the MB molecules in two ways: (i) at higher MB concentration, the larger number of MB molecules can adsorb light, which decreases the light energy absorbed by the TiO2-MCDs catalysts; (ii) the excess MB molecules were adsorbed on the TiO2-MCDs surface, suppressing the interaction of the photocatalyst with other substances such as H2O2, OH, and H2O to produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Mottet et al., 2018). We also evaluated the effect of temperature on the photocatalytic performance of the TiO2-MCDs photocatalyst using 10 ppm MB, The results indicated that the temperature dependency of the MB degradation was not significant for the investigated range of reaction temperatures (25–80 °C) (Fig. S9) because, the reaction rate remained almost constant; this result is consistent with that obtained by other researchers (Habibi et al., 2005). Therefore, room temperature can be used as the optimum temperature for the MB photodegradation by TiO2-MCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability and reusability of TiO2-MCDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: The photostability and reusability of the TiO2-MCDs composite were further evaluated by collecting and reusing the photocatalyst for four cycles (Fig. 6 ).

Compared to pristine TiO2, the TiO2-MCDs composite exhibits a high maintenance of photocatalytic activity after four rounds of testing; the photocatalytic degradation rates of MB are still maintained at cycle 2 and decrease slightly at cycles 3 and 4. The decrease during cycles 3 and 4 can be caused by the loss of the photocatalyst particles during the collection processes. These results demonstrated the potential for the practical applications of the TiO2-MCDs composite as a photocatalyst with a high photocatalytic activity. TiO2-MCDs has an equivalent photocatalytic efficiency of MB under visible light and a better stability and usability in comparison with other catalysts based on composites of TiO2 and carbon materials reported in the literature; most other TiO2-CDs composites exhibited either a high photocatalytic activity under UV light or a low photostability and reusability (Table 1 ). These results indicate that our TiO2-MCDs is a promising material for the degradation of MB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism of photodegradation reaction of MB by TiO2-MCD composite
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the photoinduced MB decomposition by TiO2 is primarily related to the redox reactions on the surface (Lee et al., 2019). TiO2 particles absorb photons in the light to generate excited electron (e−)–hole (h+) pairs for the degradation reaction of MB at their surface; the degradation is due to the production of hydroxyl radicals that are strong oxidizing agents. However, due to the high recombination rate of e− and h+ and the low light absorption capability, only ∼10% of the generated charge carriers in the pristine TiO2 can participate in the redox reaction (Schneider et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011). Therefore, we propose a possible mechanism for the enhancement of the photocatalytic MB degradation by the MCDs decorated TiO2 surface, shown in Fig. 7 . Briefly, MCDs in the TiO2-MCDs play dual crucial roles for the improved photocatalytic activity, including acting as electron reservoirs and photosensitizers. Under the irradiation of UV light, MCDs act as electron reservoirs to trap the photoinduced e− emitted from the conduction band (CB) of the TiO2 nanoparticles, facilitating the efficient separation of e− and h+ pairs (Veziroglu et al., 2020). Consequently, the electrons exhibit a shuttle movement within the conducting network of MCD, while the holes are maintained near the surface of TiO2 (Hazarika and Karak, 2016). In the second role, MCDs as photosensitizers, increase the visible light absorption of the TiO2-MCDs composite, leading to the visible-light-driven photocatalytic MB degradation. The presence of MCDs reduces the bandgap energy of TiO2 from 3.2. eV to 2.91 eV, resulting in a high visible light absorption capability of the TiO2-MCDs composite (Fig. 7a). After absorption of the photons in the visible light region, the MCDs emit a shorter wavelength of light as a result of upconversion and this high-energy light can excite TiO2 molecules to form (e−/h+) pairs (Ke et al., 2017). Due to the MCDs decoration, the TiO2-MCDs composite photocatalyst can produce a larger number of e−/h+ pairs because their recombination is hindered, and they have a higher capability to absorb visible light. The reaction of these e−/h+ pairs with H2O and O2 subsequently produces a higher yield of •OH and •O2 − radicals for the degradation of MB (Fig. 7b). In addition, the MCDs can serve as a dispersing support to control the morphology of TiO2 in the TiO2-MCDs composite because of their functional groups, thus preventing agglomeration and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 (Xu et al., 2010).

In summary, a TiO2-MCDs composite exhibiting the efficient photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV–Vis and visible light irradiation was successfully fabricated using a two-step microwave treatment method. The MCDs were prepared by the carbonization of a microalgae solution under microwave irradiation (800 W, 5 min). Thereafter, the MCDs were decorated on the surface of TiO2 via hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. We have shown that the TiO2-MCDs composite has a significantly higher MB photodegradation efficiency than that of pristine TiO2. Based on the analysis of the results, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2-MCDs under visible light irradiation is induced by the MCDs enacting two roles: (i) as electron reservoirs for trapping the photoinduced electrons and (ii) as photosensitizers for improving the absorption capability of visible light. In addition, the as-prepared composite photocatalyst exhibited a high photostability and recyclability in the photodegradation of MB. Therefore, this work is expected to provide an environmentally friendly and highly effective photocatalyst that can lead to a new development strategy for the efficient and cost-effective treatment of various organic pollutants, thereby ensuring a safe and sustainable environment.

T.T.V.N: Conceptualization, methodology, writing – original draft preparation, data curation, formal analysis; K.T.L.T.: Conceptualization, methodology, writing – original draft preparation, data curation, formal analysis; P.L.T.: data curation, formal analysis; B.T.P.: reviewing and editing, T.D.M.N: reviewing and editing; V.-P.D: reviewing and editing; T.S.P: reviewing and editing; S. G.: data curation; M.C: methodology, writing – reviewing and editing; N.H.T.T: methodology, writing – original draft preparation, data curation, formal analysis, funding acquisition; V.V.T: supervision, funding acquisition; investigation; methodology, writing – reviewing and editing, funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research (N.H.T.T and V.V.T) was supported in full or in part with a Kurita Asia Research Grant (20Pvn011-I40) provided by the Kurita Water and Environment Foundation and also supported by Vietnam National University in Ho Chi Minh City to Center for Innovative Materials and Architectures (Laboratory for Optics and Sensing) under grant NCM2019-50-01. M. Chang was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2020R1I1A306849711).
